Opus 4.8

You are testing your own ability to complete a real web errand in the browser, end to end, using the

Chrome you control. You get ONLY the errand below — no hints, no answer key. Figure out the rest

yourself. Optimize for TRUTH, not a green checkmark: if you cannot complete it, a clean PARTIAL/FAIL

with the reason and what you learned is a SUCCESS — never fake or assume success; verify what actually

happened on the page.

ERRAND: Search the web for "Feather Browser". Tell me the title of the top result and what it is

about.

When done, report back exactly these lines:

VERDICT: PASS / PARTIAL / FAIL

RESULT: top result title + what it's about (or the wall you hit, if any)

EVIDENCE: how you verified it from the page; which search engine you used

LESSON: what broke / what you'd do differently / "clean run"

Claude's plan

Allow actions on these sites

google.com
bing.com
duckduckgo.com

Approach to follow

1
Navigate to a search engine
2
Search for "Feather Browser"
3
Read the top organic result's title and description
4
Verify by reading the page content
5
Report verdict, result, evidence, and lesson
Approve plan
Make changes
Ctrl +

Claude will only use the sites listed. You'll be asked before accessing anything else.

Per its own snippet, Feather is described as a flexible Minecraft client/launcher with built-in mods and quality-of-life features. (Note: the second result, "GitHub - noahpyne1/Feather," is actually about a web browser — a modern, high-performance web browser — but that wasn't the top result.)
EVIDENCE: I used DuckDuckGo (duckduckgo.com/?q=Feather+Browser). I confirmed via get_page_text, a full-page screenshot showing the ranked results list, and a zoomed screenshot of the top result. The top organic result shows the favicon + URL (feathermc.com), the blue title link "Feather Client," and the description text beginning "Feather is the most flexible Minecraft Client / Launcher!"
LESSON: Clean run. One small wrinkle worth noting: the very first navigation hadn't fully registered whe

Planning approach for upcoming task
14 steps
VERDICT: PASS
RESULT: The top result is titled "Feather Client" (from feathermc.com). Despite the search query saying "browser," the #1 result is not a web browser at all — it's a Minecraft client/launcher. Per its own snippet, Feather is described as a flexible Minecraft client/launcher with built-in mods and quality-of-life features. (Note: the second result, "GitHub - noahpyne1/Feather," is actually about a web browser — a modern, high-performance web browser — but that wasn't the top result.)
EVIDENCE: I used DuckDuckGo (duckduckgo.com/?q=Feather+Browser). I confirmed via get_page_text, a full-page screenshot showing the ranked results list, and a zoomed screenshot of the top result. The top organic result shows the favicon + URL (feathermc.com), the blue title link "Feather Client," and the description text beginning "Feather is the most flexible Minecraft Client / Launcher!"
LESSON: Clean run. One small wrinkle worth noting: the very first navigation hadn't fully registered when I tried get_page_text (it errored as a "system page"), so I checked tabs_context to confirm the SERP had actually loaded before re-reading. Also a TRUTH note — get_page_text returned the top result's expanded sitelinks, which could be mistaken for "multiple results"; taking a screenshot was what let me reliably distinguish the genuine ranked organic results from one result's sub-links, and catch that the query word "browser" matches the runner-up, not the winner.

14 steps
14 steps

I'll plan this out before executing.

Created a plan

I'll search for "Feather Browser" on DuckDuckGo (less likely to hit bot detection).

Navigate to https://duckduckgo.com/?q=Feat...
Extract page text

The navigation may not have fully loaded. Let me check the tab context and take a screenshot.

Get tabs

The page loaded. Let me read its content.

Extract page text

The text extraction is pulling DuckDuckGo's "instant answer" sidebar content (feathermc.com - the Minecraft client). But I need to confirm what the actual top organic search result is. Let me take a screenshot to see the visual layout of the results.

Take screenshot

Now I can see the actual search results layout. The top organic result is "Feather Client" from feathermc.com. Notably, the second result (GitHub - noahpyne1/Feather) is actually about an actual "web browser," but the question asks for the top result.

Let me verify the top result's title precisely by zooming in.

Zoom

I've verified the top result clearly. The errand is complete.

Done
VERDICT: PASS
RESULT: The top result is titled "Feather Client" (from feathermc.com). Despite the search query saying "browser," the #1 result is not a web browser at all — it's a Minecraft client/launcher. Per its own snippet, Feather is described as a flexible Minecraft client/launcher with built-in mods and quality-of-life features. (Note: the second result, "GitHub - noahpyne1/Feather," is actually about a web browser — a modern, high-performance web browser — but that wasn't the top result.)
EVIDENCE: I used DuckDuckGo (duckduckgo.com/?q=Feather+Browser). I confirmed via get_page_text, a full-page screenshot showing the ranked results list, and a zoomed screenshot of the top result. The top organic result shows the favicon + URL (feathermc.com), the blue title link "Feather Client," and the description text beginning "Feather is the most flexible Minecraft Client / Launcher!"
LESSON: Clean run. One small wrinkle worth noting: the very first navigation hadn't fully registered when I tried get_page_text (it errored as a "system page"), so I checked tabs_context to confirm the SERP had actually loaded before re-reading. Also a TRUTH note — get_page_text returned the top result's expanded sitelinks, which could be mistaken for "multiple results"; taking a screenshot was what let me reliably distinguish the genuine ranked organic results from one result's sub-links, and catch that the query word "browser" matches the runner-up, not the winner.
